speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Moses Kaufman. This is tape six. Moses, you were telling us about joining the partisans. What happened when you arrived at the partisan camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived to the partisans, and I have to be interviewed through the commandant, Szczerbiec, of the partisans, Szczerbiec. It was in a little house. I was invited inside with my friend, Riszik Riszfeld. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they start to ask us, did you for any-- the only one reason that brought you here is to save your heads, that is the reason why you come to the partisans? He knows that we are Jews, of course. The only one that knows that we are Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the person that also he spoke questions. I said to him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came here, I think, to fight our enemy, the Germans. Hmm. The second question, do you know-- [CHUCKLES] do you know when we were taking Vilna, that was in 1920, liberating Vilna for Poland, he was an army-- officer in the army, General [INAUDIBLE]. That was the army that was liberated Vilna, next to Poland. That I said to him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he-- he mentioned another thing. He said, when we were-- the question was that when we were liberating Vilna from the city, [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were shooting to us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, no. Think that the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] Street, 90% of Jews or more, might be 95% of Jews, were leaving there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My answer was I finished gymnasium. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I read a lot, and I know that what we were told, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when Vilna was liberated and next to Poland, that the population were greeting the liberators with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bread and salt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He knocked on his table, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take this, what I told you. That is the truth. Shut up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The third question, the most important one what I remember, and is not to forget it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know that-- and now-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is a lot of other partisans, like the Russian partisans, the Jewish partisans, and all others, Armia Ludowa, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are fighting them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a lot of partisans are Jews there. We are fighting them. If that would be that you have to shoot them, if you will listen and we will fulfill the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Command.

speaker: interviewee
text: The command. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was thinking what else I heard this. So I said, Mr. Commandant, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know the history of Jews for the all hundreds and hundreds of years, what was going on. Jews are all over the world. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A Jew-- a Polish Jew was shooting to a German Jew, and a German Jew, vice versa. A Russian Jew was shooting to a Jewish Jew, and vice versa. Unfortunately, that our-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we are in such a situation [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that we would have to fulfill the orders. But unfortunately, we are in such a situation, the diaspora, the Jews, they are all everywhere, and one is killing the other. So there's no other way. Unfortunately, one Jew has to kill the other Jew. And he finished the interrogation, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he said to us, you are [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] able to talk another few other languages, Russian, Polish. You are very good in Polish, he said to me, and German, and so on, so on, a little bit of Lithuanian. You will be part of gendarme, by the police, by the police, the-- the Army, no, this Army police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Military police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Military police, military police. To tell you the truth, I don't know why he done this. I-- I don't believe him that he done this because we are speaking another few languages. I believe that he was trying that the people, the ranking people in the gendarme, would have-- will have us under their surveillance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your duties in the military police?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the military police, for instance, there was one factor with a German. He was caught up. And the interrogation was going through that I-- he didn't speak other languages, and I would have to, from German, to translate this in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was one of your duties?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one of my duties. The other duties have been that they send me from time to time to the town, to the-- to find out where trains are running. What they are and which direction. That was there that he sent me, as a Jew, sent me to the town to find out what kind of trains are going through. Vilna, or where they are going and what they've got on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out this information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got-- we got the people working for us. Connections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a  bit about living conditions where you were staying? Were you living-- did you have huts or tents where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was huts, mostly, in the villages, you know? But we were always changing the place where we are staying, in a radius of 15, 20 kilometers. Always-- always in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Germans occupying that particular area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were frightened to come to us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was the main purpose of the particular partisan group that you worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot was done with the fight with the Lithuanians, with the [NON-ENGLISH]. Because they were against the Poles also, very, very bad. So most of the actions was against the-- the-- what do you call it? The-- the Lithuanian collaborators.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it sabotage work? Was it direct conflict?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sabotage work. Mostly sabotage work. Mostly sabotage work. People where we were staying, the villages, they will be free of get-- no, sending the goods, their private products to this-- to the-- to the-- to the town, to Vilna. With the moment that they got paper that they give up everything from the primary produce, what they were producing, to us, so they were free of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you partake in any [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sabotage yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Happens, not so much, no. Not so much. Because we were always in the back. The police, the military police was not so much engaged. Not in the-- what do you call it, in the actions, what-- what they were putting through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say that the leader of the-- of the partisans knew that you were Jewish, and he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were Jewish and he was an anti-Semite.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was the only one who knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only one that he knew. And what happens? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, that the features of my friend, of my Riszik Riszfeld, was typical Semitic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he starts to talk inside about this. And he, most probably, he knows too that people are starting to talk about my friend. Not so much about me because my features was not so Semitic. [CHUCKLES] [INAUDIBLE]. And he called us in and told us [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he's sending us out [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about a few hundred kilometers to the south. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was another partisan group. They called them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And he told us there is a lot of you people, you people, there, so I am sending you over there. And we were sent with passports, yes? Passports. Sent to be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to go through [NON-ENGLISH], where other partisans were, the Russian partisans, Ludowa, Krajowa, Armia Krajowa, Ludowa, Jewish partisans. We have to go through them. So that was very, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- on horses and only at night. And wherever you're assigned during the day, that was already arranged. Some arrangements was done-- it was-- it took us five nights to go there. Five nights. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came over there. We stayed there two days. And the commandant from there sent us back. The same-- to go back to-- to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], to where from we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he give you reasons as to why he sent you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No reasons, nothing. Came back. And I believe the commandant from [INAUDIBLE] was frightened that something will happen to us, and he's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he's-- what do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH]. So he sent us out to get rid of us. He said, on the way there going through the all other partisans, that they will finish us off, and he will not be responsible for [INAUDIBLE] because he was his head. He was frightened for him, because he's in the scene. So he has to look after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't kill them. That didn't happen in-- in-- amongst us. That happens on the way when he was going-- and the other sent us back to-- to. True, that, in that [INAUDIBLE]. On the south of [NON-ENGLISH], a lot of Jews from Warsaw have been there. In partisan groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that partisan group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet some people from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were from Warsaw. We wouldn't have talked. We didn't went-- they didn't talk a Jew-- like a Jew to a Jew. You know, partisan? Where from? From Warsaw. But I know who was a Jew and who wasn't a Jew. I could find.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to your original partisan group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on, please

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got this enough, you know? And I went on one mission, I remember. I went to [NON-ENGLISH], and I told him about the whole story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he sent a patrol to get us out from there to come back to the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: To which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Vilna. And organizing and ask us, together with him, not far from Vilna, Valakampiai, to organize. And that was already the end of the war. Because that was, the Russians, the front was coming nearer the borders of Lithuania. To organize partisans amongst the villages in the-- Valakampiai. In Valakampiai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go onto that, just clarify something. You were in the partisan camp, and the only person who knew that you were Jewish was the leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened, do you think, had they found out, the other people in the partisan group, that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism was very high. They will finish us off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you've gone to [NON-ENGLISH], and he's put you with another partisan group. What time period was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could hear already, you know, the-- from the-- that-- high-- not the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. No. The-- we heard already that the-- the front is coming closer, you know? We could hear the-- the-- so we said to ourself, oh, the best idea would be not to sit because a lot of Germans already were still in Vilna, you know? They're all-- all-- all-- what-- run away from the front and so on, and that was not far. Valakampiai wasn't far from-- from Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said to ourself, the best thing is to-- we will postpone, postpone arrangements of organizing the partisans there. And that happens in a few days, three, four, five days, I believe, that the Russian Army took over Vilna. Where were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Valkampiai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember encountering any Russian soldiers at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian soldiers, well, yes. [CHUCKLES] There was a place they called [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Vilna, and the SS men there were barricading themselves to fight still the advanced Russian Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through loudspeakers, they were calling, the police, give up, give up, give up, you're-- you're-- give up. They didn't want. So what they done, once that was not close to the river, [NON-ENGLISH] high up. So they make the place where they could-- to show them that they could escape, you know? And a little bit further, about two kilometers further, they got a lot, you know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, they killed them. Because when I was going through the town, it was full of Germans everywhere, you know? Yeah. And it once happened that after, when they opened it, they were looking after, they called them, Fritz, Fritz, Krauts, Fritz. And I was in one where Ala was already there. That was not far from that place, Valakampiai, not far, where Ala was with that little boy, what is now in Germany, that's Urich, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was early, very early, about 6:00, 5 o'clock in the morning. One, a wild one, a mongrel one came in. Fritz, if [NON-ENGLISH], that if there are no Germans here. And he noticed I was sleeping in one little bed that was you know, a watch hanging. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he took that watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? If that is mine. I said, yes. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And took the watch and went away. But he was looking for Germans, he took my watch. [CHUCKLES] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what-- do you remember the date of when you realized that, for you, the war was, over, when the Russians had liberated Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians-- when the Russians came. The war? The war was going on. That was 1944 they liberated Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] September? What was it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Can't recollect. Can't recall it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find Ala?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with Ala.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I--

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- after--

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberation?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you about that watch, yes? And Ala was with me in that room.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Fronckiewicz was in that room. And his wife was in that room too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But when you were fighting with the partisans, and then the Russians came through, how did you find out where Ala was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly where he was during hiding, because she got documents as an Aryan. But I don't know in which, by whom she was with Lutka, together. They were together all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had it been that you had not seen or heard from Ala from when she went into hiding until when you met again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being by the partisans, [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] not by the partisans. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not clear for me. It's not clear for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing Ala for the first time when you got-- when you reunited?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Valakampiai, she was about two or three kilometers apart from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the first time you saw her after?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are things that you can't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened after this? You're together again with Ala, the Russians had occupied Vilna, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians took over again the-- the factories, the combine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Asked me to do the same work for them. And I was a technical manager, working again for the Red Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do that for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was those few months' living conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely was better. [CHUCKLES] No comparison. Not with the time in ghetto, not time in Kielce. In time, we are free people. And that happens once, a Sunday, where factories are not working. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A captain of the Army was found in Russia, Polish Army, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That was the Army that was founded there. A captain, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he recognized me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He took away some-- he was working before the war for the Department of Supply [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the Polish Army, and he took from my father [INAUDIBLE], and he recognized me. He asked me, what-- how-- how-- and I told him what I-- and he asked me what I am doing. I told him that I'm working. He said, well, if I took up the Russian citizenship. I said no, I've got the Polish citizenship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told me about this, that Bialystok was already taken because they were advancing very quickly, you know? When they're liberating part of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- they haven't got, unfortunately, the-- the specialist in the leather industry, that most, they were Jews, and so on. And unfortunately, there are not anymore. They are not existing.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you are willing to go to-- from-- to leave Lithuania. Because I didn't took up, not the Lithuanian citizenship, not the Russian citizenship, and I was a Polish citizenship. I said, by all means, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The idea was to be as close to my father to find out what happens to my family. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a general one, from [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He become-- when Bialystok was liberated, he become the [NON-ENGLISH]-- not the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't what-- how to explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: A leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a leader, no. He was the-- is a-- is a governor, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: A mayor.

speaker: interviewee
text: A mayor, a governor. A governor. A governor of Bialystok. He came twice or three times. He was coming to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Vilna, having a talk to me, having a talk to the boss. My director wasn't from the-- from the factory wasn't my boss. But the boss, mine was [INAUDIBLE], and he was the Minister of Light Industry. And there was a fight going on there to relieve me. And he relieved me to go, to leave Lithuania, to leave my position, and to go with-- to Bialystok to [INAUDIBLE] to that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to take a break now and just change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
